attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with heavy vaginal bleeding and dizziness. She reports being eight weeks pregnant based on her last menstrual period. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears pale and drowsy and unwell. Her pulse rate is 40 beats per min, blood pressure 80/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. Her abdomen is soft and non-tender. Speculum examination reveals products of conception visible at the cervical os with ongoing bleeding.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic triad of cervical shock: bradycardia, hypotension, and reduced consciousness level in the setting of products of conception in the cervical os. This represents a vagal response to cervical dilatation and mechanical stimulation of the cervix.

Cervical shock is distinguished from other forms of shock by the presence of bradycardia rather than tachycardia. The mechanism involves activation of the vagus nerve due to cervical stretching, similar to a vasovagal response. Treatment involves immediate removal of products from the cervical os to eliminate the stimulus.

Hypovolaemic shock from blood loss would typically present with tachycardia rather than bradycardia as a compensatory mechanism. While this patient does have vaginal bleeding, the bradycardia makes hypovolaemic shock less likely as the primary process.

Septic shock typically presents with tachycardia, hypotension, and often fever. There are no signs of infection in this case, making septic shock unlikely.

Cardiogenic shock results from pump failure and would be unlikely in a young woman with no cardiac history. It typically presents with signs of heart failure such as pulmonary oedema.

Neurogenic shock results from damage to the sympathetic nervous system, usually in the setting of spinal cord injury. While it can cause bradycardia and hypotension, there is no history of trauma in this case.

The immediate management should focus on removing the products of conception from the cervical os, which will typically result in rapid improvement of the patient's vital signs. Intravenous fluids and anti-cholinergic medications may be required if the response persists.
learningPoints: Cervical shock is a vagal response to products of conception in the cervical os, causing bradycardia and hypotension.
The presence of bradycardia distinguishes cervical shock from hypovolaemic shock in vaginal bleeding.
Immediate removal of products from the cervical os is essential to reverse cervical shock.
references: Birch JD, Gulati D, Mandalia S. Cervical shock: a complication of incomplete abortion. BMJ Case Rep. 2017 Jul 14;2017:bcr2017220452. doi: 10.1136/bcr-2017-220452. PMID: 28710197; PMCID: PMC5535053.
Bellomo T. Vaginal bleeding and miscarriage [Internet]. Life in the Fast Lane; 2023 [cited 2024 Nov 25]. Available from: https://litfl.com/vaginal-bleeding-miscarriage/
